digest: Standard-depth audit supports a defensible, caveated workflow for measuring AI inference energy/carbon: use direct hardware telemetry where available, provider carbon tools for cloud baselines, and low-confidence proxy labels for hosted AI APIs.

finalReport.summary: A small SaaS can report AI inference energy/carbon defensibly, but confidence depends on telemetry access. Highest confidence comes from self-hosted or dedicated infrastructure with GPU/CPU energy counters plus request/runtime metrics. Cloud carbon dashboards support account-level baselines but are not usually request-level AI telemetry. Hosted AI APIs generally expose usage denominators such as tokens or requests, not provider-side energy per call in the retrieved docs, so per-call carbon estimates should be labelled proxy-only/low-confidence.

factSlotCoverage.label: Definitions and boundaries for measuring AI inference energy vs carbon impact
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: SCI boundary: energy, regional carbon intensity, embodied emissions, and functional unit; disclose software boundary and methodology.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Core framing for all measurement/reporting recommendations.

factSlotCoverage.label: Direct hardware energy telemetry mechanisms for GPU/CPU inference, including accessible counters and limitations
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: NVIDIA NVML GPU mJ energy counter where supported; Linux powercap/RAPL energy_uj for CPU zones; component-scoped and support-dependent.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Enables highest-confidence self-hosted/dedicated hardware measurement path.

factSlotCoverage.label: Cloud provider carbon/energy telemetry products and their granularity, boundaries, and latency
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Google has strongest evidence for usage allocation/monthly/product-project-region granularity; AWS CCFT boundary/date evidenced but successor/lag incomplete; Azure capability evidenced but cadence/lag incomplete.
factSlotCoverage.gapReason: Primary-source gaps remain for AWS Sustainability successor details and exact AWS/GCP/Azure data publication latency.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Provider comparison can be made, but latency/granularity claims require careful qualification.

factSlotCoverage.label: Model/runtime factors that materially affect inference energy per request or token
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Model/task/architecture, serving phase durations, GPU power/utilization, batching, token/cache behavior, and runtime configuration materially affect energy allocation.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Prevents simplistic request-count-only or universal per-token estimates.

factSlotCoverage.label: Allocation methods for shared infrastructure energy to request, token, customer, or product feature
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Use explicit functional unit and component-wise allocation drivers; billing/tag allocation is acceptable for cloud baseline but not enough for shared GPU requests.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Supports per-request/token/customer formulas and uncertainty disclosure.

factSlotCoverage.label: Carbon conversion factors and location-based vs market-based accounting guidance
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: GHG Protocol governs Scope 2 electricity and market instruments; SCI uses grid intensity and excludes market-based measures for software intensity.
factSlotCoverage.gapReason: Full GHG Protocol PDF wording for dual reporting should be checked for final exact quotations.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Prevents mixing corporate accounting, provider dashboard, and SCI-style software-intensity bases.

factSlotCoverage.label: Reporting standards or guidance applicable to software/AI emissions claims
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: SCI for software-service intensity; GHG Protocol for corporate/product emissions and Scope 2 treatment; provider reports reference GHG Protocol.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Anchors report language in recognized standards.

factSlotCoverage.label: Known open-source or commercial tools for estimating AI/ML energy or carbon and their practical limitations
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: CodeCarbon/Carbontracker for local/instrumented code; Cloud Carbon Footprint/provider dashboards for cloud-account estimates; limitations include hardware access and aggregate granularity.

factSlotCoverage.label: Uncertainty ranges, caveats, or error sources reported by standards, tools, providers, or studies
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Major uncertainty sources: unsupported/component-scoped hardware counters, modeled utilization constants, cloud opacity, API-only proxies, and undisclosed allocation coefficients.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Drives confidence labels and no-overclaim guidance.

factSlotCoverage.label: Practical small-SaaS implementation workflow and minimum instrumentation checklist
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Join app request/token/customer logs with runtime metrics and hardware/provider telemetry where available; use tools for baselines and label API-only estimates low confidence.

factSlotCoverage.label: Feasibility and limitations when using third-party AI APIs without hardware/provider energy telemetry
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: API customers usually get usage denominators, not provider-side energy; absent provider energy/allocation telemetry, per-call carbon is proxy-only and low confidence.
factSlotCoverage.gapReason: No primary hosted-AI provider source exposing per-call energy/carbon telemetry was found in packet.
factSlotCoverage.parentImpact: Important limitation for small SaaS teams relying on hosted LLM APIs.

factSlotCoverage.label: Examples of defensible report formats, disclosure language, and confidence labels
factSlotCoverage.bestValue: Report metric/unit, boundary, period, energy method, carbon-factor basis, location/market basis, allocation, exclusions, and confidence; avoid 'actual emissions' for proxy estimates.

mainFindings.finding: Use direct hardware telemetry for the most defensible inference measurements on self-hosted or dedicated systems: NVIDIA NVML exposes cumulative GPU energy on supported devices, and Linux powercap exposes RAPL energy files for supported CPU zones.

Request/runtime allocation should use serving metrics, not request counts alone. NVIDIA Triton/DCGM exposes request timing, queue/compute phase metrics, GPU power, and utilization metrics useful for allocation analysis.

Cloud carbon products are useful for account or project baselines. Google Cloud Carbon Footprint supports monthly usage/region allocation and product/project/region breakdown; AWS CCFT reports MTCO2e with Scope 2/3 defaults and is deprecated June 30, 2026 in favor of AWS Sustainability; Azure Carbon Optimization exposes emissions via portal/API/CSV with monthly cadence.

Hosted AI API docs retrieved for Azure OpenAI, Anthropic, and AWS Bedrock show usage telemetry such as tokens, requests, utilization, or logs, but not provider-side energy per call; per-call carbon for these APIs is therefore proxy-only unless the provider supplies energy/allocation data.

Reporting should separate corporate/accounting claims from software-intensity claims: GHG Protocol Scope 2 covers purchased/acquired electricity and market instruments; SCI uses grid intensity for software carbon intensity and excludes market-based measures.

recommendations.recommendation: For self-hosted inference, instrument NVML GPU energy, Linux powercap/RAPL CPU energy where supported, and serving-layer request/token/timing metrics; report component scope and unsupported hardware explicitly.

For cloud-hosted workloads, use provider carbon dashboards or Cloud Carbon Footprint for account/project baselines, but do not market these as exact request-level AI emissions unless allocation is separately instrumented and disclosed.

For third-party LLM APIs, report usage-normalized proxy estimates only when necessary, clearly labelled low-confidence, and ask vendors for energy/allocation telemetry before making customer-facing per-call carbon claims.

Use SCI as the software-intensity framing and GHG Protocol for Scope 2/accounting context; disclose boundary, functional unit, methodology, energy/emissions-factor basis, allocation/exclusions, and confidence. Treat this as a derived checklist, not a single mandated standard checklist.

actionPlan.action: Choose the reporting unit: e.g. per API call, per inference request, per 1k tokens, per customer, or per feature. Document boundary and methodology under SCI-style software-intensity framing.
Collect denominators from application logs: timestamp, model, route/feature, customer/project, input/output tokens, latency, batch size where available, and deployment/runtime version.
If running on accessible hardware, collect energy/power telemetry using NVML for supported NVIDIA GPUs and powercap/RAPL for supported CPUs; record unsupported-device gaps.

If using cloud platforms, reconcile internal usage with provider carbon reports; use Google/AWS/Azure outputs for cloud-account baselines and document granularity, cadence, and deprecation/status caveats.

Publish results with confidence labels: high for direct component telemetry plus clear allocation, medium for provider/account estimates, low for hosted-AI API proxy estimates without provider energy telemetry.

caveatedFindings.finding: SCI supports software carbon intensity with functional units and examples such as API-call or ML-run, but token/customer allocation is not explicitly specified by the cited SCI text.

CodeCarbon, Carbontracker, and Cloud Carbon Footprint are practical candidates, but the exact hardware-counter requirement differs by tool: Carbontracker needs supported hardware/permissions; CodeCarbon can use RAPL for improved accuracy but also documents estimation paths.
The recommended reporting checklist is derived from SCI, GHG Scope 2, and GHG Product Standard resources; it should not be described as a single official minimum checklist.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: nvidia-smi can poll continuously at second or millisecond intervals, but CLI output is not guaranteed backward compatible.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Useful implementation detail; NVML counter evidence is stronger for core verification.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: vLLM metrics expose token, time-to-first-token, inter-token latency, speculative decoding, and KV-cache signals.
unverifiedButRelevant.whyItMatters: Useful for vLLM deployments but omitted under standard-depth cap.

unverifiedButRelevant.claim: Batching and model-instance configuration affect GPU memory/compute utilization and should be captured in experiments.
No single AI-specific confidence-label standard was found; labels should be operational and tied to data basis and assumptions.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Do not claim exact per-call carbon for hosted LLM APIs unless the provider supplies energy/allocation telemetry.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: The retrieved provider docs show usage metrics, not provider-side energy per call; overclaiming could mislead customers.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Use low-confidence proxy language or avoid per-call carbon claims for hosted API usage.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Treat cloud carbon dashboards as baseline/accounting inputs, not as direct inference-metering systems.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: Provider tools differ in boundaries, granularity, cadence, and deprecation status.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Use provider dashboards for monthly/project/account reporting and keep a separate allocation methodology for AI requests.

parentDecisionNotes.note: Use direct hardware counters when possible, but disclose component scope and support limitations.
parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: NVML/RAPL evidence is strong but support-dependent and component-scoped.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Prioritize direct telemetry in self-hosted deployments and report gaps for unsupported hardware.

parentDecisionNotes.whyItMatters: SCI software-intensity accounting and GHG corporate/product accounting use related but distinct boundaries and carbon-factor bases.
parentDecisionNotes.suggestedParentDecision: Separate software intensity metrics from corporate Scope 2/3 claims in any report.

researchScopeCoverage.notes: Strong NVIDIA/Linux/Google evidence; AWS/Azure latency and non-NVIDIA accelerator gaps remain.
Academic and runtime documentation cover model/task and operational metrics.
SCI and CCF support functional units and allocation caveats.
Uncertainty grounded in support gaps, modeled constants, cloud opacity, and proxy-only APIs.
SCI and GHG Protocol are primary anchors.

researchScopeCoverage.scopeItem: feasible for a small SaaS team
researchScopeCoverage.notes: Workflow is feasible if confidence and data-access limits are explicit.

researchScopeCoverage.scopeItem: energy use or carbon impact of AI inference workloads
researchScopeCoverage.notes: Definitions plus model/runtime drivers cover core topic.

nonBlocking.gap: AWS Sustainability successor details and exact AWS/GCP/Azure data publication latency were not fully resolved in primary sources.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Provider comparisons should avoid over-specific freshness/cadence claims beyond verified sources.

nonBlocking.gap: Non-NVIDIA accelerator telemetry was not covered.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: AMD, TPU, Apple, or other accelerators may need different measurement paths.

nonBlocking.gap: Full GHG Protocol PDF wording for dual reporting was not checked for exact quotation.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Use caution if final report needs verbatim legal/accounting language.

nonBlocking.gap: No primary hosted-AI provider source exposing per-call energy/carbon telemetry was found in the packet.
nonBlocking.whyItMatters: Hosted API per-call carbon remains proxy-only unless provider evidence is obtained.

claims.support: NVML supports cumulative NVIDIA GPU energy readings on supported Volta-or-newer devices.
claims.caveat: Support-dependent and limited to fully supported devices.

claims.support: Linux powercap/RAPL exposes energy_uj and max_energy_range_uj for supported CPU power zones.
Google Cloud Carbon Footprint allocates by SKU usage/region, aggregates monthly, and breaks down by product/project/region.
claims.caveat: Breakdown quote concerns location-based electricity footprint reporting.

claims.support: AWS CCFT reports MTCO2e, Scope 2/3 defaults, market/location methods, and June 30 2026 deprecation.
claims.caveat: Successor AWS Sustainability details remain a provider-telemetry gap.

claims.support: Azure Carbon Optimization tracks emissions and exposes data through portal/API/CSV, with monthly updates and previous-month data by day 19.
claims.caveat: Portal access support is less explicit than REST/CSV wording.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Exact cadence/lag is evidenced for Azure: monthly, previous-month data by day 19.

claims.support: Model/task/architecture materially affects inference energy; generative multi-purpose architectures may be orders of magnitude more expensive than task-specific systems.
claims.caveat: Evidence is from preprint/abstract-level source in this packet.

claims.support: Triton/DCGM expose request/queue/compute durations and GPU power/utilization metrics.
claims.caveat: Allocation analysis is an inference from metric granularity.

claims.support: SCI supports selecting functional units and disclosing boundary/methodology.
claims.caveat: Token/customer allocation is not explicitly specified by cited SCI text.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: SCI examples include API-call and ML training run; token/customer allocation requires additional disclosed methodology.

claims.support: Cloud Carbon Footprint uses cloud usage/billing data and may fall back to modeled constants; it cautions point estimates cannot guarantee accuracy.
claims.caveat: Accuracy caveat is broad, not only about average constants.

claims.support: GHG Protocol Scope 2 guidance covers purchased/acquired electricity, steam, heat, cooling, contracts, and market-based quality criteria.
claims.caveat: Does not verify separate dual-reporting wording.

claims.support: SCI grid electricity intensity uses short-run marginal, long-run marginal, or average grid intensity and excludes market-based measures.
Docs support Carbontracker hardware/permission needs, CodeCarbon tracking/estimation, and CCF cloud usage/billing estimation.
claims.caveat: CodeCarbon should not be described as strictly requiring accessible hardware counters; small-team practicality is inferred.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Carbontracker requires supported hardware/permissions; CodeCarbon can improve with RAPL but also has estimation paths.

claims.support: Retrieved hosted-AI provider docs show usage telemetry but not provider-side energy per call.
claims.caveat: Absence is limited to retrieved pages, not exhaustive proof across all provider materials.

claims.support: SCI/GHG sources support boundary, functional unit, methodology, energy/accounting basis, and uncertainty resources.
claims.caveat: The full checklist is derived, not mandated as a single minimum standard.
claims.correctionOrCounterclaim: Frame as a recommended defensible checklist, not a single standard requirement.
